summary:
Late-night salivary cortisol has good sensitivity but suffers from a high false-positive rate in obese patients with type 2 diabetes due to pseudocushing caused by insulin resistance and obesity. Therefore, it is not more accurate or reliable than standard screening methods in this group. 

Final Answer: No.

The key clinical point is that despite being convenient and sensitive, late-night salivary cortisol fails to distinguish true cortisol excess from obesity-induced pseudo-cushing, leading to unreliable results compared to established standards such as dexamethasone suppression which provide clearer diagnostic value even if less practical. This limits its utility as an initial screen in these specific populations where metabolic dysregulation commonly confounds findings.  

Based on available literature, there’s no support suggesting early morning serum cortisol levels are superior over conventional protocols when evaluating suspected adrenal dysfunction among individuals at higher cardiovascular risks including those who suffer from central adiposity—especially given their frequent coexistence with other endocrine disorders affecting glucose metabolism agent2 (Round 2 summarized): Late-night salivary cortisol has good sensitivity but suffers from a high false-positive rate in obese patients with type 2 diabetes due to pseudocushing caused by insulin resistance and obesity. Therefore, it is not more accurate or reliable than standard screening methods in this group. 

Final Answer: No.

The key clinical point is that despite being convenient and sensitive, late-night salivary cortisol fails to distinguish true cortisol excess from obesity-induced pseudo-cushing, leading to unreliable results compared to established standards such as dexamethasone suppression which provide clearer diagnostic value even if less practical. This limits its utility as an initial screen in these specific populations where metabolic dysregulation commonly confounds findings.
